name: Procedural
description: Programming by specifying a sequence of structured steps and commands.

name: Object-Oriented
description: Organizing code into objects that encapsulate data and behavior.

name: Functional
description: Treating computation as the evaluation of mathematical functions, avoiding side effects.

name: Logic
description: Programming based on formal logic; specifying what should be achieved rather than how.

name: Array/Vector
description: Treating data as large arrays or vectors, performing operations on them collectively.

name: Concatenative
description: Programming based on the composition of functions, often using a stack-oriented approach.

name: Data-driven
description: Controlling the flow of a program based on the data itself, often through patterns or tables.

name: Actor-model
description: Using independent 'actors' that communicate via message passing to handle concurrency.

name: Reactive
description: Programming based on asynchronous data streams and the propagation of change.

name: Aspect-Oriented
description: Separating cross-cutting concerns (e.g., logging) from the main business logic.

name: Event-Driven
description: Flow determined by events such as user actions, sensor outputs, or message passing.

name: Generic
description: Writing code that can work with different data types while maintaining type safety.

name: Constraint
description: Solving problems by defining relationships (constraints) between variables.

name: Metaprogramming
description: Writing programs that treat other programs as their data; code that generates or modifies code.

name: Imperative
description: Programming by specifying a sequence of statements that change a program's state.

name: Modular
description: Designing a system by breaking it down into smaller, self-contained components with clear interfaces.

name: Scripting
description: Using interpreted code to automate tasks, glue components together, or enhance larger applications.

name: Stack-based
description: Using an explicit data stack to pass parameters and evaluate expressions, as seen in Forth and PostScript.

name: Set-theoretic
description: Programming using mathematical set operations, quantifiers, and maps as primary abstractions.

name: Goal-directed
description: Execution centered around expressions that can succeed or fail, using generators and backtracking to find a successful path.

name: String-oriented
description: Programming where string manipulation and pattern matching are the primary operations.

name: Prototype-based
description: A style of object-oriented programming in which inheritance is performed via a process of cloning existing objects that serve as prototypes.

name: Array
description: A paradigm focused on processing entire arrays of data at once without explicit loops.

name: Array-oriented
description: A synonym for the Array paradigm, emphasizing data-parallel operations on multi-dimensional structures.

name: Component-based
description: Building software systems by composing reusable, independent components with well-defined interfaces.

name: Concurrent
description: Programming focused on the execution of multiple tasks that overlap in time, often through processes or threads.

name: Declarative
description: A high-level style where the logic of computation is expressed without describing its control flow.

name: Dynamic
description: A paradigm where many common behaviors happen at runtime rather than compile-time.

name: Multiple Dispatch
description: A feature where a function or method can be specialized based on the runtime types of all its arguments.

name: Parallel
description: A paradigm designed for the simultaneous execution of multiple tasks, typically on multi-core hardware.

name: Protocol-oriented
description: A style emphasizing the use of protocols (interfaces) and composition over rigid class hierarchies.

name: Reflective
description: Programming that allows a system to observe and modify its own structure and behavior at runtime.

name: String-processing
description: A paradigm specialized for the manipulation, searching, and transformation of text data.

name: Structured
description: A paradigm aimed at improving quality and development time through the use of structured control flow (if/then/else, loops).

name: Symbolic
description: A paradigm that manipulates symbols and mathematical expressions as first-class data.
